chain_of_thought:
Native stack shows strlen_default -> Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode -> fuzz_one_input. Crash is in strlen_default, typically due to missing NUL in string or invalid pointer.
We successfully decompiled Java Base64.decode(String). It calls a native _decode(str) method (JNI function Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode) which presumably fills this.byteBuff and returns an int status; if status <=0 returns byteBuff, else throws exception.
The crashing native frame is that JNI Base64 decode function, not the X509Certificate one (which is listed in lib map and JNI bridge line, but not in stack for this crash). The fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input most likely calls into Java Base64.decode with arbitrary fuzzer-controlled strings, which then reach JNI decode.
strlen_default being the termination reason suggests the native code calls strlen on some pointer derived from the Java string or its bytes, without validating its content (e.g., assuming ASCII/Base64 and NUL-termination) or mismanaging buffers. Under fuzzing, the harness probably passes arbitrary bytes including embedded NUL or not properly-formed UTF-16->UTF-8 conversions, or even NULL pointer, causing strlen to read out of bounds and trigger allocator abort.
However, Base64 decoding is inherently user-controlled in real app use: any input string passed to Base64.decode (e.g., from certificates, network, files) will reach the same JNI layer. If there are insufficient checks before passing to strlen, malformed yet syntactically valid Java strings could trigger similar behaviour outside fuzzing.
Missing Ghidra decompilation due to 403 prevents direct inspection of the JNI function, but the pattern (JNI Base64 decode using strlen_default internally, crashing there) strongly indicates an out-of-bounds read or invalid pointer deref in libc strlen caused by unsafe assumptions on input. That is a classical memory safety vulnerability with potential DoS and maybe more, depending on surrounding code.
Since the pointer to strlen is ultimately derived from Java string data, which is attacker-controlled in many realistic contexts, and there is no indication of thorough validation, we treat this as a likely vulnerability. Severity is high (memory corruption/DoS), confidence moderate-high (~0.7) because we cannot see exact native code, but the call stack and function naming strongly support it.
We cannot map exact addresses or snippets of libDSToolkitV30Jni.so, therefore evidence will be higher-level. Call sequence is: fuzz_one_input -> Java Base64.decode (Java) -> JNI Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode -> strlen_default (libc).
reasons: Crash occurs in strlen_default invoked from JNI Base64 decode function, indicating unsafe string length computation on attacker-controlled input
Java Base64.decode forwards arbitrary input string to native _decode without visible validation beyond checking for null/empty
Same native code will be reachable in normal app flows wherever Base64 decoding is used, so malformed inputs could trigger similar crashes outside fuzz harness
recommendations: Disassemble/decompile Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode in libDSToolkitV30Jni.so and inspect all uses of strlen/memcpy/memmove with pointers derived from Java strings
Introduce explicit length tracking and bounds checks for input strings before calling strlen or similar functions in the native Base64 decoder
Add defensive checks in Java layer (e.g., restrict size and character set of Base64 strings) and create regression tests with malformed inputs that previously caused this crash
assumptions: Fuzz harness passes arbitrary strings into Base64.decode similarly to how user/network input would in production
The pointer passed to strlen in the native decode function is derived from or points into attacker-controlled input buffers
There is no hidden validation/sanitization in the unseen native code that would entirely prevent malformed strings from reaching strlen
limitations: Unable to decompile libDSToolkitV30Jni.so due to tooling access error, so exact code pattern is inferred from stack trace and typical usage
No direct visibility into fuzz harness implementation or how fuzz_one_input maps bytes to Java String
Stack trace does not show instruction addresses, limiting precise mapping to Ghidra analysis

exploit.trigger_method: Pass specially crafted or malformed Base64-like strings into com.dreamsecurity.dstoolkit.util.Base64.decode from an input vector (network, file, IPC)
prerequisites: Attacker can influence a string that the app passes to Base64.decode
App does not prevalidate or strongly constrain the Base64 input format
exploit_pipeline: Supply malformed or extremely long Base64 string as input to the app feature that uses Base64.decode
Ensure this string is propagated unchanged to com.dreamsecurity.dstoolkit.util.Base64.decode
Trigger the decoding operation, causing native JNI Base64 decoder to run and call strlen on an unsafe buffer
exploit.notes: With direct native code access, a more precise PoC could be constructed targeting the exact buffer and length assumptions leading to strlen_default crash.
